Hero.lead: Gregg Pavitt has led PFM reform for Ministries of Finance across 15 countries — designing the systems, building the capacity, and delivering the assessments donors rely on. The same field-tested IPSAS expertise is now available as a self-serve Drill kit or authoritative live Desk reference.

twoWays.eyebrow: Two ways to work with us
twoWays.headline: Senior PFM expertise on the ground, or in your daily workflow.
twoWays.consultBody: For ministries and donors needing senior PFM expertise on the ground — embedded advisory, system design, capacity building, and the assessments donors rely on.
twoWays.toolkitBody: For teams who need IPSAS Drills and IPSAS Desk in their daily work, at a fraction of consulting cost. Self-serve, 14-day free trial, no card to look around.

Bio.headline: Reliable delivery in the toughest PFM environments.
Bio.tagline: PFM Specialist · 25+ Years · Pacific, Africa, Asia
Bio.paragraph1: Gregg Pavitt is a public financial management specialist with over 25 years of experience helping governments in Sub-Saharan Africa, South Asia, and the Pacific strengthen how they plan, allocate, and account for public resources.
Bio.paragraph2: He provides technical assistance and advisory services in six core areas: PFM studies and assessments, public expenditure management (PEM), domestic revenue mobilisation (DRM), integrated financial management systems (IFMIS), public investment management (PIM), and International Public Sector Accounting Standards (IPSAS).
Bio.paragraph3: Gregg has served as Team Leader and Key Expert on programmes funded by the EU (EuropeAid), World Bank, DFAT, USAID, and DFID/FCDO — both in long-term embedded roles within Ministries of Finance and on targeted short-term assignments. He is known for effective policy dialogue with government counterparts, practical reform design, and building national capacity that outlasts the project cycle.
Bio.paragraph4: International consulting firms and development organisations engage Gregg for his ability to mobilise quickly, manage multi-disciplinary teams, and deliver reliably within the compliance and reporting frameworks that donor-funded programmes demand.
Bio.quote: “Gregg was the consummate professional consultant on my team…His advice was measured, clearly expressed and possessed both longer-term strategic vision for what could be possible and the short-term operational steps to be implemented immediately.”

Expertise.headline: Six reform tracks, one integrated practice.

pfm-studies.label: PFM Studies
pfm-studies.description: Conducting diagnostic assessments (PEFA, fiduciary reviews) and research to inform PFM reform strategies.

ifms.label: IFMS
ifms.description: Advising on the design, implementation, and improvement of government financial information systems.

ipsas.label: IPSAS
ipsas.description: Supporting governments transitioning to accrual-basis accounting and IPSAS-compliant financial reporting.

pim.label: PIM
pim.description: Strengthening systems for planning, prioritizing, and executing public investment programs to maximize development outcomes.

pem.label: PEM
pem.description: Supporting budget formulation, execution, and reporting reforms to improve fiscal discipline and service delivery.

drm.label: DRM
drm.description: Helping governments increase domestic revenue through tax policy, administration reform, and compliance improvement.

fsm.title: Creating and Deploying IPSAS based Reporting System
fsm.desc: Developing custom IPSAS reporting system. Modifying secondary chart of accounts, automating power pivot production system for multi-year reporting of governmental, donor, and special purpose funds.

eu-drm.title: PFM Diagnostic Studies
eu-drm.desc: Delivered tax reform analysis, fiscal transparency assessments, and local revenue modeling across Mozambique and Malawi — including PEFA reviews, VAT and corporate tax reform, property tax restructuring, and subnational fiscal decentralization for the Millennium Challenge Corporation.

ifmis.title: IFMIS design and operationalisation
ifmis.desc: Design and procure a resilient IFMIS to modernize public financial management, ensure GAAP compliance, and maximize transparency across island operations. Coordinated for individual island states and national government.

LeadMagnet.metaTitle: Free download — IPSAS Adoption Checklist for MoF
LeadMagnet.metaDescription: A practical 12-point checklist covering the steps a Ministry of Finance must complete before going live with accrual IPSAS. Free download.
LeadMagnet.headline: IPSAS Adoption Checklist for Ministries of Finance
LeadMagnet.lead: A 12-point checklist covering the steps you must complete before going live with accrual IPSAS. Drawn from engagements across the Pacific and Sub-Saharan Africa.

checklist.item1: Lock the data model — chart of accounts mapped to IPSAS line items before any system change.
checklist.item2: Inventory the standards in scope — first-time adoption uses IPSAS 33 plus the standards effective in your transition year.
checklist.item3: Identify exemptions you will use under IPSAS 33 — and the deadlines for closing each one.
checklist.item4: Capture the opening balance — measurement basis decided per asset class.
checklist.item5: Decide your transition disclosures — reconciliation from previous basis to IPSAS.
checklist.item6: Map your IFMIS to the new line items — including budget line mapping (IPSAS 24).
checklist.item7: Train preparers and reviewers — minimum 40 hours per finance officer.
checklist.item8: Document accounting policies — short-form policy manual approved by the CFO.
checklist.item9: Walk through one full reporting cycle in parallel — old basis and IPSAS side-by-side.
checklist.item10: Get audit pre-engagement — auditor sign-off on opening balances before going live.
checklist.item11: Set the cut-over date — and the date by which all amendments must be closed.
checklist.item12: Plan the post-implementation review — month 6 and month 12.

form.downloadLead: Enter your email below. The download link appears immediately on this page. No follow-up unless you opt in.
form.consent: Send me the checklist and (occasional) IPSAS practice notes. I can unsubscribe any time.
form.privacyNote: We use your email to send the download and, if you opt in, occasional practice notes. You can unsubscribe at any time. We never share or sell email addresses.

Desk.metaDescription: Sourced, paragraph-cited IPSAS answers for government finance teams and audit firms. Built for IPSAS first, not IFRS with public-sector mode. Free trial.

hero.eyebrow: Now in beta · For practitioners and audit teams
hero.headline: IPSAS Desk
hero.tagline: The technical reference desk for teams without one.
hero.lead: Ask any IPSAS question in plain English. Get a sourced answer with the exact paragraph citation, written so it can go straight into your working papers. Built specifically for IPSAS — not IFRS with public-sector content bolted on.

what.headline: Twenty seconds. Paragraph reference. Plain English.
what.p1: A senior PFM advisor bills somewhere between USD 800 and USD 2,000 per day. For most public-sector accounting questions, that's the wrong tool — too expensive, too slow, and unavailable when you need an answer in time for the close.
what.p2: The Desk is the right tool for those questions. Ask in plain English. Upload supporting documentation if it helps. You get back the applicable standard, the specific paragraph(s), the recommended treatment for your context, and — where it matters — a note on what working-paper documentation an auditor will expect.
what.p3: Every answer is grounded in the actual IPSAS source text. Citations are real and verifiable. The Desk is designed never to invent a paragraph reference that doesn't exist.

ipsasFirst.headline: Built for IPSAS first, not IFRS with public-sector mode.
ipsasFirst.p1: Most AI accounting tools were built for the IFRS / FASB market and added IPSAS later, if at all. The result: shallow handling of the standards that matter most in public sector — IPSAS 23 (non-exchange revenue), 24 (budget reporting), 32 (service concession arrangements), 35 (consolidated statements), and the GFS reconciliation that ties government accounts back to fiscal reporting.
ipsasFirst.p2: The Desk is built around those standards, by a practitioner who applies them on real engagements. The IFRS-equivalent treatments are there too, where they help — but the centre of gravity is public sector.

steps.step1Body: Tell the Desk your jurisdiction, entity type, reporting basis (cash, modified accrual, full accrual), and which IPSAS standards you've adopted. Answers are tuned to your situation, not generic.
steps.step2Body: “We received a USD 4m grant from a bilateral donor with conditions tied to construction milestones. How do we recognise this under IPSAS 23?” The Desk identifies the applicable standard, reads any documents you upload, and answers in context.
steps.step3Body: Paragraph citations, recommended treatment, suggested working-paper note. Copy what you need into your file. The citation is verifiable — it points to a real paragraph in a real standard.

audience.headline: Built for teams who need a defensible answer fast.

cards.card1Title: Senior accountants and CFOs in government
cards.card1Body: For when the question is too hard for the team but too small to bring in a consultant. Get a sourced answer fast, defend it to your auditor, move on.
cards.card2Title: Audit firms outside the Big Four
cards.card2Body: For firms that audit governments but don't have a public-sector technical desk. The Desk fills that role: ask, cite, document, sign.
cards.card3Body: For consultants who need to give a defensible answer quickly to a Ministry counterpart, with the citation already attached.

limits.headline: What the Desk doesn't do.
limits.p1: The Desk is a research and reference tool, not a replacement for professional judgment or audit. It will give you a sourced position on an IPSAS question; you and your team are still responsible for the accounting decision and the audit response.
limits.p2: It also doesn't pretend to know what it doesn't know. If a question requires entity-specific information the Desk doesn't have, it asks. If a question falls outside IPSAS into local statute or regulation, it says so — unless you're on a Customised plan, where local statute and regulation can be added to the Desk's reference base.

trust.headline: Sourced answers. Built by a practitioner, not a chatbot vendor.
trust.lead: The Desk is curated and operated by Gregg Pavitt, a public financial management specialist with 25 years of work across Sub-Saharan Africa, South Asia, and the Pacific. The reference base is the actual IPSAS standards — IPSAS 1 through the most recently issued — plus IPSASB framework documents and selected public-sector application guidance. Updates are pushed as standards are revised.
trust.donorsHeadline: Donors funding the underlying consulting practice

pricing.headline: Try it free. Subscribe when you've used it on a real question.
pricing.p1: Start with a 14-day free trial — generous query allowance, no credit card needed to look around. Add a card to keep going past day 14.
pricing.p2: For audit firms, ministries, and donor-funded organisations, see team and organisation pricing. Customised plans (your local statute and regulation added to the reference base) are available for organisations that need them.

faq.headline: Common questions
faq.q1: How is this different from ChatGPT or general AI tools?
faq.a1: ChatGPT will confidently cite IPSAS paragraph numbers that don't exist. The Desk is grounded in the actual standards: every citation is traceable to a real paragraph. If the answer isn't in the source, the Desk says so.
faq.q2: How is this different from IFRS Buddy / Acclara / IFRS Companion?
faq.a2: Those tools are excellent at IFRS. They handle IPSAS as a secondary feature, if at all. The Desk is built for IPSAS first.
faq.q3: Can it handle our local jurisdiction?
faq.a3: Standard plans answer in IPSAS terms with general public-sector context. Customised plans add your local statute, regulations, and accounting manual to the reference base, so the answer reflects your specific compliance environment.
faq.q4: Will the answer hold up in an audit?
faq.a4: The citation will. The accounting position is yours and your auditor's to agree on — but the Desk gives you a defensible, sourced starting point with the paragraph reference your auditor will ask for anyway.
faq.q5: What if I'm just learning IPSAS?
faq.a5Part1: The Desk is built for practitioners with a working knowledge. If you're learning the standards,

cross.headline: Drills + Desk go together.
cross.body: Drills builds your team's reflexes on routine treatments. Desk handles the hard cases when they come up.

Drills.metaDescription: Field-tested IPSAS practice for government finance teams and audit firms. Built by a current Pacific Treasury implementation lead, not from a textbook. Free trial.

hero.eyebrow: Now in beta · Pacific, Sub-Saharan Africa, South Asia
hero.headline: IPSAS Drills
hero.tagline: Build the reflexes. Close the books faster.
hero.lead: Short, focused practice questions tuned to the situations your team will actually face — opening balances that won't reconcile, donor grants under IPSAS 23, GFS mapping with a chart of accounts that wasn't designed for it. Built from current implementation work in a Pacific Treasury, not from a textbook.

whyNot.eyebrow: Why this isn't another IPSAS course
whyNot.headline: This isn't a course. It's training the way professionals actually train.
whyNot.p1: Most IPSAS training is built around the standards. You read the standard, you watch the video, you sit the exam. That's how you get a certificate — but it's not how you get faster, more accurate, or more confident on a Monday morning when the books need closing.
whyNot.p2: Drills works the way a junior surgeon, a pilot, or an auditor in a Big Four firm trains: short, repeated, focused on the moves you'll actually make. Ten to fifteen minutes a day. Targeted at the situations that come up in real public-sector accounting — not edge cases, not exam tricks, not US-only treatments.

painPoints.headline: Built around the problems that actually stop IPSAS adoption.
painPoints.lead: Each drill is graduated by difficulty and tagged to the standard, but organised around real implementation pain points:
painPoints.card1Body: The unglamorous work that derails most IPSAS adoptions. First-time recognition of assets and liabilities. Restating prior-year comparatives. Opening balance sheet construction when source data is patchy.
painPoints.card2Body: Where small governments live or die. IPSAS 23 (non-exchange revenue) at the level you'll actually apply it. Grant conditions, restrictions, and timing. Consolidating donor-funded special purpose funds into the whole-of-government accounts.
painPoints.card3Body: What auditors actually look at. Whole-of-government consolidation under IPSAS 35. GFS-to-IPSAS mapping. Cash-flow statement construction in environments where the IFMIS doesn't help. Disclosure quality.

topics.headline: 15 topics. Every standard your team will actually meet.
topics.lead: The pain points above are where most teams start. Here's the full topic coverage — each tagged to the standards that govern it.

items.DT-01-tagline: The biggest balance sheet item — and the biggest source of audit qualifications.
items.DT-02-tagline: Where IPSAS most differs from commercial accounting.
items.DT-03-tagline: Pension and leave obligations done properly, with actuarial method.
items.DT-04-tagline: Legal claims, guarantees, and PPP obligations — usually under-disclosed.
items.DT-05-tagline: Defining the reporting entity, and the mechanics of consolidating it.
items.DT-06-tagline: Original budget, final budget, actual — with the variance narrative auditors want.
items.DT-07-tagline: Concessional loans, ECL staging, and government loan portfolios.
items.DT-08-tagline: Recoverable service amount for schools, hospitals, and roads.
items.DT-09-tagline: Putting PPP assets on balance sheet, where they belong.
items.DT-10-tagline: Distribution stock, strategic reserves, and donated goods.
items.DT-11-tagline: Right-of-use assets and lease liabilities under IPSAS 43.
items.DT-12-tagline: The four primary statements, accounting policies, and prior-period errors.
items.DT-13-tagline: First-time recognition, deemed cost, and comparative restatement.
items.DT-14-tagline: Multi-year grants, in-kind contributions, and clawback risk.
items.DT-15-tagline: Whole-of-government consolidation, GFS mapping, and audit working papers.

howItWorks.headline: Three steps. Ten minutes a day.
howItWorks.step1Body: Tell us your role and what you're working on. We'll start you with drills appropriate to your situation, not generic exam prep.
howItWorks.step2Body: Ten to fifteen minutes per session. Each question gives you the answer, the standard, the paragraph, and — where it matters — the implementation note from real projects.
howItWorks.step3Body: Solo users see their own progress. Team and ministry plans give the lead accountant a dashboard: who's drilling, where the team is weakest, and what to focus on next.

audience.headline: Built for the teams who actually have to make IPSAS work.
audience.card1Body: For Ministries, line ministries, and statutory bodies adopting or applying IPSAS. Especially useful where the team is small, capacity is stretched, and external training is hard to access.
audience.card2Body: For small and mid-size firms where government audit isn't the firm's main practice. Bring your team's IPSAS knowledge up to a level where you can confidently sign the opinion.
audience.card3Body: For staff supporting government counterparts on IPSAS reform. Use Drills yourself; deploy it to the teams you're supporting.

trust.headline: Built by someone currently doing the work.
trust.lead: Drills is written and curated by Gregg Pavitt — a public financial management specialist with 25 years of experience across Sub-Saharan Africa, South Asia, and the Pacific, and currently embedded in the Solomon Islands Ministry of Finance & Treasury. The questions come from the implementations he is running and has run, with input from the counterparts who have lived the problems.
trust.donorsHeadline: Donors funding the underlying consulting practice

pricing.headline: Try it free. Subscribe when it's earning its keep.
pricing.p1: Start with a 14-day free trial — no credit card needed to look around. Add a card to keep going past day 14. Cancel any time before the first charge.
pricing.p2: For teams of 5 or more, ministries, or audit firms wanting a single account for the whole staff, see team and organisation pricing.

faq.headline: Common questions
faq.a1: Drills isn't a certificate program — that's CIPFA and ACCA's territory, and they do it well. Drills makes you better at the daily work. Many teams use Drills alongside a CIPFA Certificate or Diploma, not instead of one.
faq.q2: Is this aligned to the current IPSAS standards?
faq.a2: Yes — Drills covers IPSAS 1 through the most recent issued standards, including IPSAS 41 (Financial Instruments), 43 (Leases), and the public-sector-specific standards (23, 24, 32). Updates are pushed as standards are revised.
faq.q3: We're a Ministry — can we get a single account for the whole finance team?
faq.a3: Yes. Ministry plans cover unlimited staff in one entity, with a dashboard for the Director of Accounts. We also offer donor-funded pricing for governments where adoption is being supported by an international donor.
faq.q4: What if I want one-on-one help on something specific?
faq.a4Part2: is for. Drills builds reflexes; Desk handles the hard cases.

cross.headline: Drills + Desk go together.
cross.body: Drills builds your team's reflexes on routine treatments. Desk handles the hard cases when they come up.

Pricing.metaDescription: Pricing for IPSAS Drills and IPSAS Desk. Self-serve plans for individuals and small teams; organisation plans for ministries and audit firms; donor-funded and regional pricing available.

hero.headline: Pricing
hero.tagline: Built to be affordable to the teams that need it most.
hero.lead: Self-serve plans for individuals and small teams. Organisation plans for ministries and audit firms. Donor-funded pricing for governments adopting IPSAS with international donor support. All prices in USD; reduced pricing available for organisations in low- and lower-middle-income countries — see below.

drillsSection.headline: For staff and teams building IPSAS reflexes.
drillsSection.donorNotePart2: If your IPSAS adoption is being supported by World Bank, EU, DFAT, USAID, MCC, FCDO, or another donor,
drillsSection.donorNotePart3: our donor-funded pricing format — designed to drop directly into a project budget line.

deskSection.headline: For practitioners and audit teams who need sourced answers, fast.

bundle.headline: Save 20% when you take both.
bundle.lead: Drills builds the reflexes for routine treatments. The Desk handles the hard cases when they come up. Most of our team and ministry customers want both — so we offer them together at a discount.

regional.headline: Regional and donor-funded pricing
regional.p1: We charge full list price to organisations in high-income countries. For organisations based in countries on the OECD DAC list of ODA recipients — particularly Least Developed Countries and Lower-Middle-Income Countries — we offer reduced pricing of 30 to 50 percent off list, depending on country tier and organisation type.
regional.p2: For governments adopting IPSAS with the support of an international donor (World Bank, EU, DFAT, USAID, MCC, FCDO, and others), we provide pricing in a format designed to drop directly into a project budget line. We will provide a sample budget justification on request.
regional.p3: To request regional or donor-funded pricing, send us a brief on your organisation, country, and intended use, and we'll come back to you within two working days.

faq.headline: Common questions about pricing
faq.q1: Do prices include VAT or local taxes?
faq.a1: Prices are exclusive of any local sales tax or VAT. For invoiced organisational plans, applicable taxes will be shown on the invoice.
faq.q2: Can we pay by invoice rather than card?
faq.a2: Yes — Team, Organisation, Firm, and Customised plans can all be invoiced annually in advance. Individual and Practitioner plans are card-only.
faq.a3: Yes. Self-serve monthly plans cancel at the next billing date. Annual plans run to the end of the period; we don't pro-rate refunds, but we don't auto-renew without a reminder.
faq.q4: What counts as one “query” on the Desk?
faq.a4: One query is one question with one answer, including any follow-up clarification within the same conversation. Practitioner plans have a generous 100/month allowance; in normal use, very few practitioners reach the cap.
faq.a5: Both products offer a 14-day free trial with no credit card required to start. We don't offer a permanently free tier — keeping the products focused, accurate, and well-maintained requires real revenue.

deskProduct.summary: Ask any IPSAS question in plain English. Get a sourced answer with the exact paragraph citation, written so it can go straight into your working papers. Built specifically for IPSAS — not IFRS with public-sector content bolted on.
deskProduct.f1: Plain-English questions; paragraph-cited answers from the IPSAS source
deskProduct.f2: Context-aware — jurisdiction, entity type, reporting basis per conversation
deskProduct.f3: PDF document upload (up to 4MB) for transaction-specific questions
deskProduct.f4: Working-paper export format on Firm and above
deskProduct.f5: Customised plans add your local statute and regulation
deskProduct.f6: Conversation history saved for audit reference
deskProduct.tier1Detail: Up to 100 queries / month. Or USD 480 / year (save USD 108).
deskProduct.tier2Detail: Up to 10 named users sharing 1,000 queries / month. Annual invoicing.
deskProduct.tier3Detail: Local statute, regulations, and accounting manual added to the reference base.

faq.headline: Common questions
faq.a1: Ministries of Finance, national treasuries, and audit offices adopting or already using IPSAS. Jurisdiction overlays available for Solomon Islands; others on request.
faq.a2: Row-level security on every table. Org admins see only their org. Correct answers are never returned to the browser — answer-checking runs server-side with a service role key.
faq.a3: The advisor cites only paragraphs retrieved from the IPSAS knowledge base. Citations can be hovered to see the source text. If no paragraph is found, the advisor is instructed to say so rather than guess.
faq.q4: What LLM does the advisor use?
faq.a4: Routed via OpenRouter: DeepSeek R1 for primary reasoning, Gemini 1.5 Flash for citation verification, GPT-5-nano for summaries. Admins can change the model per task.
faq.q5: Is my org's data used to train any model?
faq.a5: No. Conversations are stored in your org's Supabase tables only. No data leaves the hosting infrastructure for model training.

pilot.headline: Pilot programme is open.
pilot.body: Eligible ministries get a 14-day pilot with full access to both products. Send a brief and Gregg will respond within two working days.
